disclosure_items.clinical_fact: Widespread thick, raised red plaques with dense silvery-white scale over the extensor elbows and knees, lower back and scalp, spreading to the trunk
trigger_question_examples: Can you describe the patches and where they are?
What does the affected skin look like?
disclosure_items.patient_response_template: They're thick raised red patches covered in silvery flakes, mainly on my elbows, knees, lower back and scalp, but now spreading across my trunk too.

disclosure_items.clinical_fact: Skin over the plaques is rigid and tight and fissures into deep painful cracks that bleed on movement, causing functional immobility
disclosure_items.patient_response_template: The skin is so tight it cracks open when I bend my arms or legs, and then it bleeds and hurts terribly — I've basically been stuck in bed.

disclosure_items.clinical_fact: Known 10-year history of the same skin condition, previously confined to elbows and knees, now dramatically worse
trigger_question_examples: Is this a known skin problem for you?
disclosure_items.patient_response_template: Yes, I've had psoriasis for about 10 years, usually just on my elbows and knees, but it's never been anything like this widespread or painful.

disclosure_items.clinical_fact: Flare followed a recent streptococcal throat infection and a period of severe psychosocial stress
trigger_question_examples: Was there anything going on before it flared — an illness or a lot of stress?
Have you been unwell recently, for example a sore throat?
disclosure_items.patient_response_template: Now that you mention it, I had a nasty throat infection a couple of weeks ago, and I've been under a huge amount of stress — it all seemed to kick off after that.

disclosure_items.clinical_fact: A new plaque developed at the site of a recent scratch on the forearm (isomorphic response to skin trauma)
trigger_question_examples: Have any new patches appeared where the skin was injured or scratched?
Did a patch come up anywhere it wasn't before?
disclosure_items.patient_response_template: Yes — a new patch came up exactly where I scratched my forearm a few days ago.

disclosure_items.clinical_fact: Systemically well — no fever, no rigors/shaking chills, feels normal temperature; the red scaly patches are discrete with normal skin in between and are NOT red over the whole body (pertinent negatives against erythroderma and sepsis)
trigger_question_examples: Do you have any fever or shivering, and do you feel unwell in yourself?
Is your skin red all over your whole body, or is it in separate patches?
disclosure_items.patient_response_template: No fevers or shakes at all, I feel okay in myself apart from the skin. It's in separate thick patches with normal skin between them — I'm not red all over.

disclosure_items.clinical_fact: No pus-filled blisters, no yellow weeping/oozing or crusting, and no honey-coloured discharge from the cracks (pertinent negatives against pustular psoriasis and secondary bacterial infection)
trigger_question_examples: Are there any pus-filled blisters or spots?
Is any of the cracked skin weeping, oozing yellow fluid or crusting?
disclosure_items.patient_response_template: No little pus spots or blisters, and nothing is weeping or oozing yellow — the cracks just bleed a bit when I move.

disclosure_items.clinical_fact: Has largely stopped the previously prescribed topical calcipotriol/betamethasone because the involved area is now too vast to cover
trigger_question_examples: Are you using any creams or treatments for it at the moment?
What have you been prescribed before, and are you still using it?
disclosure_items.patient_response_template: I've got a calcipotriol/steroid ointment from before, but I've pretty much stopped it — there's just way too much skin to cover with it now.

disclosure_items.clinical_fact: High distress from severe pain and being bed-bound, with worry about the sudden dramatic spread
trigger_question_examples: How are you coping with all this?
How is this affecting you day to day?
disclosure_items.patient_response_template: I'm really struggling — the pain is constant, I can't move around, and I'm frightened by how fast it spread everywhere.
